title: Building Chatbots
lesson:
A chatbot feels magical to use, but under the hood it is surprisingly simple plumbing. The model itself has no memory and no idea who you are. Every time you press send, your app must hand the model the entire conversation again, from scratch. The chatbot is the wrapper that remembers the conversation, formats it correctly, calls the model, and streams the answer back to the screen.

Think of the model like a brilliant goldfish. It can write a sonnet or debug your code in seconds, but the instant it answers, it forgets everything. To make it feel like a continuous chat, you keep a notebook (the conversation history) and read the whole notebook aloud to the goldfish before every single question. That re-reading is the core trick of every chatbot ever built.

The message list is the whole game

Modern chat models expect a list of messages, where each message has a role (who is speaking) and content (what they said). There are three roles you will use constantly: system (your standing instructions to the bot), user (what the human typed), and assistant (what the bot said before). The System Prompts message is like the rules taped to a new employee's desk: tone, job, and what not to do. It is sent first, every time.

(In the snippets below, MODEL is just whatever model name your provider expects, hosted or local: the message-list call works the same either way.)

messages = [
  {"role": "system", "content": "You are a friendly support bot for AcmeShoes."},
  {"role": "user", "content": "Where is my order #4471?"},
  {"role": "assistant", "content": "Let me check that for you."},
  {"role": "user", "content": "Thanks, how long does it take?"},
]
reply = client.chat.completions.create(model=MODEL, messages=messages)

To continue the chat, you append the model's reply to the list and send the whole thing again next turn. That growing list is the conversation. There is no hidden state on the model's side. This is the single most important idea in this whole topic: a chatbot does not hold a conversation, it rebuilds the conversation from scratch every time you press send. Once that clicks, everything else is just decoration on top of one list.

Memory and the context window

Here is the catch: the list cannot grow forever. The model can only read so many Tokens (chunks of text, roughly 3-4 characters each) at once. That limit is the Context Windows. When a long chat overflows it, you must manage memory yourself. The usual moves: trim (drop the oldest turns), summarise (replace old turns with a short recap), or use a proper AI Memory Systems to store and recall facts later.

It is like a chef working from a recipe card that fits only ten lines. As you add steps, the early ones scroll off the bottom. A good chef rewrites the card to keep only what still matters. Your chatbot must do the same.

Grounding the bot in real facts

A raw model will confidently make things up (a hallucination is an invented but plausible-sounding answer). For a real product, you almost always bolt on RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): before answering, you search your own documents, then paste the best matches into the prompt as context. Now the bot answers from your help-centre articles instead of guessing. To do things (look up an order, refund a payment), you give it Tool Calling so it can call your functions and use the results. Picture a new call-centre hire who is brilliant at talking but knows nothing about your company: RAG is the binder of policies you slide across the desk, and tools are the login that lets them actually open the order system. Without both, even a smart bot is just a confident stranger.

Streaming and the feel of speed

Nobody wants to stare at a spinner for eight seconds. Chatbots stream the answer token by token, so words appear as they are generated, like water filling a glass rather than arriving all at once. This does not make the model faster, but it makes the wait feel far shorter, which is a real Latency vs Quality Tradeoffs lever you control.

stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model=MODEL, messages=messages, stream=True)
for chunk in stream:
    delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
    print(delta, end="", flush=True)  # words appear live

Put it together and a production chatbot is: a system prompt for behaviour, a message list for memory, a context-window strategy so it never overflows, RAG for facts, tools for actions, and streaming for feel. Whether you call a hosted API Serving endpoint or run a model on your own box with Ollama, the message-list pattern is identical. Master that one loop and you can build almost any chatbot.
mentalModel: The model is a brilliant goldfish with no memory. Your chatbot is the notebook that re-reads the whole conversation to it before every question, plus the plumbing that grounds it in facts and streams the answer back.
mistakes: Forgetting to resend history. Sending only the latest user message makes the bot amnesiac. You must pass the full (or summarised) message list every turn, since the model keeps no state between calls.
Letting the conversation overflow the context window. Long chats silently break or get truncated. Trim or summarise old turns before you hit the token limit.
No system prompt, or a vague one. Without clear standing instructions the bot drifts in tone and scope. Be specific about its job, voice, and what it must refuse.
Trusting the model for facts. Asking it for your prices, policies, or order data without RAG or a tool call invites confident hallucinations. Ground every factual claim in retrieved data.
Ignoring streaming. Returning the whole answer at once feels slow and broken to users. Stream tokens so the reply appears as it is written.
No guardrails on user input. Pasting raw user text straight into the prompt lets people override your instructions (prompt injection). Validate input and keep system rules separate and authoritative.
exercise:
Build a tiny command-line chatbot in about 30 lines. Keep a Python list called messages that starts with one system message defining a persona (say, a sarcastic pirate cook). In a loop: read the user's typed input, append it as a user message, call the chat API, print the reply, and append the reply back as an assistant message so the next turn remembers it. Have a real three-turn conversation and confirm it recalls earlier answers.

Stretch goal: Add a simple memory cap. When the list grows beyond, say, 10 messages, drop or summarise the oldest user/assistant pairs (never the system message) so the chat keeps working in a long session without overflowing the context window.
solution:
The loop is the key. Each turn appends two messages: messages.append({"role":"user","content":text}), then after the call messages.append({"role":"assistant","content":reply}). Because you resend the whole list, turn 3 still sees turns 1 and 2, which is why it remembers.

For the stretch goal, keep the first item fixed and trim the middle: if len(messages) > 10: messages = [messages[0]] + messages[-8:]. A nicer version asks the model to summarise the dropped turns into one short system note so no important facts are lost.
summary: A chatbot is plumbing around a stateless model: a system prompt sets behaviour, a growing message list gives memory, and you resend it every turn. Manage the context window by trimming or summarising, ground answers with RAG, act via tool calling, and stream tokens so it feels fast.

keyTerms.def: The ordered list of role/content messages that makes up the conversation; you resend it to the model on every turn because the model itself remembers nothing.
A label on each message saying who is speaking: system (your instructions), user (the human), or assistant (the bot's past replies).
The first, standing-instruction message that sets the bot's job, tone, and rules. Sent every turn so behaviour stays consistent.
The maximum amount of text (counted in tokens) the model can read at once. When a chat exceeds it, you must trim or summarise old turns.
Sending the answer back token by token as it is generated, so words appear live instead of after one long pause. Improves perceived speed, not actual speed.
A confident, plausible-sounding answer the model invents when it lacks real information. Reduced by grounding the bot in retrieved facts.
Feeding the model real source material (via RAG or tools) so its answers are based on your data instead of guesses.
When user-supplied text tries to override your system instructions. A reason to validate input and keep system rules separate and authoritative.
